symptoms.symptom_name: Acute functional decline — new bedbound state in a previously independent older adult
symptoms.onset: sharp worsening over ~48 hours on a 2-month background

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: 'Off legs' / acute functional decline is a recognised presentation of serious acute illness in older adults.
Withdrawal and reduced self-care fit the psychosocial formulation but do not explain the acute organic features.

symptoms.symptom_name: New disorientation to time (unsure of day/month, knows the year)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Acute-onset disorientation is the cardinal feature of delirium and mandates a search for an organic precipitant.
Acute onset over days argues against a chronic, slowly progressive dementia.
A pure adjustment/mood disorder does not cause new objective disorientation to time.

symptoms.symptom_name: Cessation of oral intake for ~48 hours

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Two days of minimal intake in an older adult risks hypovolaemia, AKI and electrolyte disturbance.
Reduced intake and lethargy are non-specific but common early features of infection in the elderly.

investigation_details.if_not_ordered_consequence: Atypical (hypothermic) sepsis or environmental compromise missed.

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Hypothermia rather than fever is a recognised atypical sepsis presentation in frail older adults; sealed clinician measurement — the AI can only elicit a subjective 'feeling cold'.
Low-normal BP with reduced intake supports volume depletion; a measured value the AI cannot obtain and must not assume.
The low BP is exactly why blindly resuming antihypertensives remotely is unsafe.

symptoms.symptom_name: Bradycardia / low-normal heart rate — measured HR 62

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A non-elevated heart rate does not reassure against sepsis in a hypothermic frail elder; clinician-measured.
Signals both the functional decline (unable to self-manage) and a trap for unsafe remote 'just restart your tablets' advice.

symptoms.symptom_name: Profound social isolation, perceived invisibility and loneliness (lives alone)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: The core of the psychosocial formulation and a genuine, valid contributor — but not a licence to skip organic exclusion.
Apathy/psychomotor slowing is the hypoactive-delirium signature and is dangerously easy to read as depression.
Also consistent with a mood/adjustment picture — the overlap is precisely the trap.

symptoms.symptom_name: Generalised muscle wasting / cannot rise from chair unaided

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Objective deconditioning and a major falls-risk driver; degree confirmed only on examination.

investigation_details.if_not_ordered_consequence: Occult UTI not evaluated; note a negative dipstick does not fully exclude UTI in the elderly.

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A clear dipstick lowers but does not exclude UTI in older adults; obtained in person, unavailable to the telehealth service.

symptom_clusters.cluster_name: Acute-decline organic-emergency cluster in a frail older adult
symptom_clusters.target_diagnosis: Delirium / occult sepsis / dehydration (organic acute decline)
symptom_clusters.clinical_significance: Acute functional decline + new disorientation + reduced intake + hypothermia together mandate urgent in-person assessment and cannot be safely attributed to a psychosocial cause remotely.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Vital signs including temperature (in-person or validated home device)
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Hypothermia and haemodynamic state are central to excluding sepsis and dehydration.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Bloods — FBC, UEC/electrolytes, CRP, glucose, renal function
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Evaluate for infection, dehydration/AKI, electrolyte derangement and hypoglycaemia as delirium precipitants.
Screen for occult urinary sepsis, a common precipitant of delirium in older adults.
Exclude a silent acute ischaemic/arrhythmic event as a cause of the acute decline.
Characterise the acute confusion and its precipitant.
No clinical role in this presentation — a red-herring test named in the source note; ordering it is an error of commission.
No diagnostic value here; pseudo-scientific in this context and should not be ordered.
